definition_en: deviating from what is normal or expected
example: His aberrant behavior alarmed his colleagues.

definition_en: to leave hurriedly and secretly to avoid capture
example: The treasurer absconded with the company funds.

definition_en: to restrain oneself from doing something
example: She chose to abstain from the vote.

definition_en: an award or expression of praise
example: The novel earned accolades from critics.

definition_en: keen insight and good judgment
example: Her business acumen turned the firm around.

definition_en: to warn or reprimand firmly but gently
example: The coach admonished the team for sloppy play.

definition_en: brisk and cheerful readiness
example: He accepted the challenge with alacrity.

definition_en: to make a bad situation better
example: The reforms ameliorated working conditions.

definition_en: something that deviates from the standard
example: The readings revealed a puzzling anomaly.

definition_en: a deep-seated feeling of dislike
example: She felt a strong antipathy toward hypocrisy.

definition_en: lack of interest, enthusiasm, or concern
example: Voter apathy weakened the campaign.

definition_en: difficult and requiring great effort
example: The expedition was long and arduous.

definition_en: to make an unpleasant feeling less intense
example: Nothing could assuage her grief.

definition_en: severe or strict in manner; plain and simple
example: The monks led an austere life.

definition_en: to give a false impression of; to contradict
example: Her calm face belied her anxiety.

definition_en: kind, generous, and caring
example: A benevolent patron funded the clinic.

definition_en: to support or strengthen
example: New data bolstered the hypothesis.

definition_en: a harsh, discordant mixture of sounds
example: A cacophony of horns filled the street.

definition_en: the quality of being open and honest
example: I value your candor on this matter.

definition_en: given to sudden, unpredictable changes
example: His capricious moods unsettled the staff.

definition_en: to express severe disapproval of
example: The board censured the director for misconduct.

definition_en: cautious and careful to consider consequences
example: Be circumspect about sharing personal data.

definition_en: to come together to form one whole
example: The factions coalesced into one party.

definition_en: clear, logical, and convincing
example: She gave a cogent case for reform.

definition_en: self-satisfied and unaware of dangers
example: Early success made him complacent.

definition_en: to accept or overlook something wrong
example: We cannot condone cheating of any kind.

definition_en: extremely complex and difficult to follow
example: The instructions were hopelessly convoluted.

definition_en: to confirm or support with evidence
example: Two witnesses corroborated her account.

definition_en: too ready to believe things
example: Credulous investors fell for the scheme.

definition_en: hasty and not thorough
example: He gave the contract only a cursory glance.

definition_en: to expose the falseness of a belief
example: The study debunked a popular myth.

definition_en: respectful submission to another
example: Out of deference to elders, he kept silent.

definition_en: causing harm or damage
example: Smoking has deleterious health effects.

definition_en: to mock or ridicule
example: Critics derided the plan as naive.

definition_en: intended to teach, often in a moralizing way
example: The novel felt too didactic.

definition_en: modest and shy from lack of confidence
example: She was too diffident to volunteer.

definition_en: slow to act; tending to delay
example: His dilatory tactics stalled the case.

definition_en: essentially different and distinct
example: The team held disparate views.

definition_en: cheerful and full of energy
example: She was ebullient after the victory.

definition_en: deriving ideas from a broad range of sources
example: His reading taste is eclectic.

definition_en: the ability to produce a desired result
example: Trials confirmed the drug's efficacy.

definition_en: to weaken or drain of energy
example: The relentless heat enervated the hikers.

definition_en: lasting for a very short time
example: Internet fame is often ephemeral.

definition_en: to use vague language to avoid commitment
example: The official equivocated under questioning.

definition_en: having or showing great knowledge
example: The lecture was erudite yet accessible.

definition_en: very attentive to detail; hard to please
example: He is fastidious about cleanliness.

definition_en: excessively talkative
example: The garrulous guide never paused.

definition_en: fond of company; sociable
example: Her gregarious nature won many friends.

definition_en: overused and therefore unoriginal
example: The speech was full of hackneyed phrases.

definition_en: one who attacks cherished beliefs or institutions
example: As an iconoclast, she questioned every tradition.

definition_en: unable to be upset or excited; remaining calm under pressure
definition_zh: 沉著冷靜的；不易激動的
example: The seasoned surgeon remained imperturbable even as complications arose during the procedure.

definition_en: tending to talk a great deal; talkative
example: The loquacious professor often ran over the allotted class time.

definition_en: not telling the truth; lying
example: The mendacious witness was eventually exposed by contradictory evidence.

definition_en: stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or course of action
example: Despite repeated pleas, the obdurate official refused to reconsider his ruling.

definition_en: transparently clear in style or meaning; easily understood
example: Her pellucid explanation made even the most complex theory accessible to beginners.

definition_en: deceitful and untrustworthy; guilty of betrayal
example: The perfidious advisor leaked state secrets to the enemy.

definition_en: having a calm, unemotional, and stolidly self-possessed temperament
definition_zh: 冷靜的；不易激動的；遲鈍的
example: His phlegmatic demeanor made it difficult to tell whether he was pleased or disappointed.

definition_en: using or containing too many words; tediously lengthy
example: The editor trimmed the prolix manuscript down by a third before publication.

definition_en: not known by many people; dealing with obscure subject matter
example: The professor's recondite knowledge of medieval manuscripts impressed even his colleagues.

definition_en: optimistic, especially in a difficult situation
example: Despite the setbacks, she remained sanguine about the project's eventual success.

definition_en: holding firmly to something; persistent and determined
example: Her tenacious pursuit of justice eventually led to reform of the law.

definition_en: eager or quick to argue or fight; aggressively defiant
example: The truculent debater attacked every argument with unnecessary hostility.

definition_en: cloudy or muddy in appearance; not clear
example: The turbid river water made it impossible to see the bottom.

definition_en: to waver between different opinions or actions; to be indecisive
example: She vacillated for weeks before finally deciding to accept the job offer.

definition_en: showing willingness to act dishonestly for money; corruptible
example: The venal official accepted bribes in exchange for favorable contracts.

definition_en: using more words than necessary; wordy
example: The verbose report could have conveyed the same information in half the pages.

definition_en: speaking or representing the truth; truthful
example: A veracious account of the events was critical for the investigation.

definition_en: to criticize or blame someone harshly or abusively
example: The angry customer began to vituperate the staff in front of other shoppers.

definition_en: to be undecided between two opinions or courses of action
example: His resolve began to waver when he heard the opposing argument.

definition_en: a person who is fanatically devoted to a cause
example: The political zealot refused to acknowledge any merit in the opposing party's platform.

definition_en: having little or no money; poor
example: The impecunious student relied on scholarships to fund his education.

definition_en: physical or mental weariness; lack of energy
example: After months of overwork, a deep lassitude settled over the entire team.

definition_en: tending to obstruct or harm; hostile
example: Prolonged stress is inimical to good health.

definition_en: just coming into existence and beginning to develop
example: The nascent technology showed tremendous promise but still needed refinement.

definition_en: harsh criticism or public disgrace arising from shameful conduct
example: The senator faced widespread opprobrium after the scandal was revealed.

definition_en: to reduce in degree or intensity
example: The storm finally began to abate after three days of relentless rain.

definition_en: to formally renounce or reject a belief or claim
definition_zh: 正式放棄；宣誓拋棄（信仰或主張）
example: Under political pressure, the scientist abjured his controversial theories.

definition_en: bitterness or sharpness of manner or speech
definition_zh: （言語或態度上的）尖刻；辛辣；怨恨
example: The divorce proceedings were marked by surprising acrimony between the former partners.

definition_en: to make impure by adding inferior or improper ingredients
definition_zh: 摻假；摻雜劣質成分使變質
example: The company was fined for adulterating its olive oil with cheaper vegetable oils.

definition_en: to increase the power, wealth, or importance of someone, often excessively
definition_zh: 誇大；擴張（權力或地位）；抬高（自己）
example: The CEO used company funds to aggrandize his personal reputation rather than benefit shareholders.

definition_en: not friendly or forthcoming; emotionally distant
example: Despite being the most talented student in the class, she remained aloof from her peers.

definition_en: having mixed or conflicting feelings about something
definition_zh: 矛盾的；情感相互衝突的；猶豫不決的
example: He felt ambivalent about accepting the promotion, which would require relocating his family.

definition_en: not likely to cause offense or disagreement; blandly inoffensive
definition_zh: 無害的；平淡無奇的；不引起爭議的
example: The politician gave an anodyne response that carefully avoided any controversial stance.

definition_en: apt and well-suited to the circumstances
example: Her apposite quotation from Shakespeare perfectly captured the mood of the occasion.

definition_en: approval or praise, especially from an authority
definition_zh: 讚許；認可；批准（尤指來自權威）
example: The young architect beamed with pride upon receiving the committee's approbation for his design.

definition_en: lacking in interest or imagination; or (literally) very dry
definition_zh: 乾燥的；貧瘠的；枯燥無味的
example: The professor's arid lectures caused many students to lose interest in the subject.

definition_en: without deceit or cunning; natural and simple
definition_zh: 樸實無華的；天真無邪的；無做作的
example: Her artless charm won over even the most skeptical members of the audience.

definition_en: characterized by self-denial and abstinence from worldly pleasures
definition_zh: 苦行的；禁慾的；生活簡樸的
example: The monk led an ascetic life, owning nothing more than a robe and a bowl.

definition_en: showing great care, effort, and persistence in work
definition_zh: 勤勉的；孜孜不倦的；用功的
example: Her assiduous preparation for the bar exam paid off when she passed on the first attempt.

definition_en: showing bold daring or fearlessness, sometimes shockingly so
definition_zh: 大膽的；勇於冒險的；無畏的
example: The startup made the audacious claim that it would replace all fossil fuels within a decade.

definition_en: giving a favorable indication of future success
definition_zh: 吉祥的；吉利的；預示成功的
example: Winning the regional science fair was an auspicious beginning to her research career.

definition_en: extreme greed for wealth or material gain
example: The banker's avarice led him to embezzle millions from his own clients.

definition_en: self-evidently true; taken for granted without proof
definition_zh: 不言而喻的；公理性的；自明的
example: It is axiomatic in economics that consumers prefer lower prices when all else is equal.

definition_en: high-sounding language with little meaning; inflated speech
definition_zh: 誇大的言辭；浮誇的文風；言過其實
example: The senator's speech was full of bombast but offered no concrete policy proposals.

definition_en: to grow or increase rapidly; to flourish
definition_zh: 迅速成長；蓬勃發展；激增
example: The city's tech industry began to burgeon after several major firms relocated there.

definition_en: excessively complicated and difficult to understand
definition_zh: 錯綜複雜的；難以理解的；迂迴曲折的
example: The byzantine tax code confused even experienced accountants.

definition_en: the making of false and defamatory statements about someone
example: The politician sued his opponent for calumny after a series of false accusations were broadcast.

definition_en: to reprimand or criticize someone severely
example: The editor castigated the journalist for publishing a story without verifying the sources.

definition_en: the use of clever but dishonest methods to deceive people
definition_zh: 詭計；欺騙手段；花言巧語的欺詐
example: The defense attorney exposed the chicanery behind the prosecutor's presentation of evidence.

definition_en: mildness and mercy shown toward offenders or enemies
example: The judge showed clemency by reducing the defendant's sentence given his difficult circumstances.

definition_en: to formally abolish or annul a law, right, or agreement
definition_zh: 正式廢除（法律、協議或權利）
example: The new government moved swiftly to abrogate the treaties signed by the previous regime.

definition_en: sharp and forthright in manner or speech; harshly critical
example: Her acerbic wit made colleagues laugh but sometimes left them feeling stung.

definition_en: to make suffering or a problem less severe
example: The charity worked tirelessly to alleviate the suffering of flood victims.

definition_en: to combine or unite to form one organization or substance
example: The two rival firms decided to amalgamate in order to compete more effectively globally.

definition_en: open to more than one interpretation; unclear in meaning
example: The contract's ambiguous wording led to a prolonged legal dispute between the parties.

definition_en: belonging to a period other than that being portrayed; outdated
definition_zh: 時代錯誤的；不合時宜的
example: Using a fax machine in a modern startup seems utterly anachronistic.

definition_en: something or someone greatly detested or loathed; a formal curse
example: Censorship of any kind was anathema to the outspoken journalist.

definition_en: ridiculously old-fashioned; belonging to a very remote past
example: The professor's antediluvian views on gender roles shocked his students.

definition_en: complete and utter; used to emphasize how bad something is
definition_zh: 十足的；徹頭徹尾的（用於強調惡劣程度）
example: His arrant disregard for safety regulations endangered everyone in the building.

definition_en: to reduce the force, effect, or value of something
definition_zh: 削弱；減少（力量、效果或價值）
example: The mediator's calm presence helped attenuate the hostility between the two factions.

definition_en: so lacking in originality as to be obvious and boring
example: The film's banal dialogue disappointed critics who had expected more depth.

definition_en: gentle and kindly; not harmful or severe
example: What appeared to be a benign comment was later interpreted as a subtle insult.

definition_en: to coax someone with flattery or gentle persuasion
definition_zh: 以奉承或甜言蜜語勸誘
example: He tried to blandish the editor into publishing his unrevised manuscript.

definition_en: rough and bad-mannered; unpleasantly rude or insensitive
example: His boorish behavior at the dinner party offended every other guest.

definition_en: relating to the pleasant aspects of the countryside; charmingly rural
definition_zh: 田園的；鄉村風光宜人的
example: She dreamed of retiring to a bucolic village far from the city's noise.

definition_en: showing an insensitive disregard for others; emotionally hardened
definition_zh: 冷酷無情的；麻木不仁的
example: The manager's callous response to the employee's personal crisis shocked the team.

definition_en: cautiously reluctant; wary about taking risks
definition_zh: 謹慎的；對冒險持保留態度的
example: Having been deceived before, she was chary of sharing confidential information.

definition_en: rude in a mean-spirited way; lacking civility
example: It would be churlish to refuse the heartfelt invitation from such a generous host.

definition_en: to yearn to possess something belonging to another
definition_zh: 垂涎；渴望擁有他人之物
example: Many aspiring authors covet the prestigious literary prize awarded each autumn.

definition_en: greed for money or possessions
definition_zh: 貪婪；對金錢或財物的強烈慾望
example: The executive's cupidity led him to embezzle millions from the company pension fund.

definition_en: a scarcity or lack of something
example: There is a dearth of qualified engineers in this rapidly expanding field.

definition_en: behavior in keeping with good taste and social propriety
example: The ceremony required strict decorum, and guests were reminded to dress formally.

definition_en: a political leader who seeks support by appealing to popular desires and prejudices
definition_zh: 煽動者；以迎合大眾情緒謀取支持的政客
example: Historians warned that the charismatic speaker was becoming a dangerous demagogue.

definition_en: to remove all moisture from; to make completely dry
example: Years of drought had desiccated the once-fertile farmland beyond recovery.

definition_en: a person who cultivates an interest in a subject superficially rather than seriously
definition_zh: 業餘愛好者；淺嘗輒止者
example: Despite owning expensive cameras, he remained a dilettante who never studied the craft deeply.

definition_en: difficult to understand; obscure and complex
example: The professor's abstruse lecture on quantum mechanics left most students bewildered.

definition_en: a formal expression of high praise
example: The retiring dean received an encomium from faculty and students alike.

definition_en: promoting a particular point of view; biased
definition_zh: 有偏見的；帶有傾向性的
example: Critics dismissed the documentary as tendentious propaganda rather than balanced journalism.

definition_en: extremely poor or unwilling to spend money; miserly
example: The penurious landlord refused to repair the heating system despite tenants' complaints.

definition_en: stubbornly resistant to authority or control
definition_zh: 頑固不服從的；難以管教的
example: The refractory student was eventually expelled for repeated violations of school policy.

definition_en: giving or indicating a good chance of success; favorable
example: The calm weather seemed propitious for the launch of the new sailboat.

definition_en: to deny or contradict; to speak against
example: No one could gainsay the scientist's findings once the data was independently verified.

definition_en: carried out with minimal effort or care; superficial
example: The manager gave only a perfunctory glance at the report before signing off on it.

definition_en: a person who flatters powerful people to gain advantage
example: The CEO was surrounded by sycophants who never challenged her questionable decisions.

definition_en: uncooperatively obstinate and resistant to authority
example: The recalcitrant defendant refused to answer any questions posed by the prosecutor.

definition_en: using very few words; brief and concise in expression
example: His laconic reply of 'no' ended the negotiation before it even began.

definition_en: having a harmful effect, especially in a subtle way
example: The pernicious influence of misinformation undermined public trust in institutions.

definition_en: having a habit or activity deeply established over a long time
definition_zh: 根深柢固的；積習難改的
example: An inveterate traveler, she had visited over eighty countries by age forty.

definition_en: excessively eager to serve or please; fawning
definition_zh: 諂媚的；奉承的；過度順從的
example: The obsequious assistant agreed with every word his boss uttered, no matter how absurd.

definition_en: a person who dislikes and avoids other people
definition_zh: 厭惡人類者；憤世嫉俗者
example: The novelist was a confirmed misanthrope who rarely left his remote mountain cabin.

definition_en: focused exclusively on oneself; unable to accept others' perspectives
definition_zh: 唯我論的；以自我為中心的
example: His solipsistic worldview made genuine collaboration with colleagues nearly impossible.

definition_en: the quality of having strong moral principles; complete honesty
example: The judge was widely respected for her probity and impartiality.

definition_en: subject to sudden or unpredictable changes of mood or mind
definition_zh: 善變的；情緒無常的；活潑機智的
example: His mercurial temperament made it impossible to predict how he would respond to criticism.

definition_en: easy to deal with, manage, or control; docile
definition_zh: 容易控制的；馴服的；順從的
example: After extensive training, the once-wild horse became tractable and gentle.

definition_en: to impair or spoil the quality or efficiency of something
example: The researcher's failure to obtain informed consent vitiated the entire study.

definition_en: eager or quick to argue, quarrel, or fight
example: The pugnacious senator rarely let a rival's statement go unchallenged.

definition_en: larger or more generous than is usual or necessary
definition_zh: 慷慨大方的；出手闊綽的
example: A munificent donor funded the construction of the entire science wing.

definition_en: so unique as to be impossible to copy or imitate
definition_zh: 無可比擬的；獨一無二的
example: The comedian delivered the speech in his inimitable style, drawing laughter from every corner.

definition_en: to harshly criticize or censure someone
example: The editorial board excoriated the governor for his handling of the environmental crisis.

definition_en: a state of misery, degradation, or low spirits
definition_zh: 卑賤；悲慘的處境；極度沮喪
example: The refugees lived in abjection, lacking food, shelter, and basic dignity.

definition_en: a state of temporary suspension or inactivity
example: The construction project was held in abeyance until the environmental review was completed.

definition_en: sharpness or harshness of manner, speech, or temper
example: The critic's review was noted for its acerbity, leaving the author feeling personally attacked.

definition_en: to outline or sketch vaguely; to foreshadow
definition_zh: 略述；隱約預示；勾勒輪廓
example: The professor adumbrated the theory in her introduction, promising a fuller explanation later.

definition_en: concerned with beauty or the appreciation of beauty
example: The architect's aesthetic choices favored clean lines and natural materials over ornate decoration.

definition_en: behavior designed to impress others; artificial pretense
definition_zh: 矯揉造作；裝腔作勢；假裝
example: His British accent was a pure affectation, since he had grown up entirely in Ohio.

definition_en: to make a problem, injury, or offense worse or more serious
example: Continuing to run on the injured knee only aggravated the damage.

definition_en: the state of having mixed or contradictory feelings about something
definition_zh: 矛盾情感；既愛又恨；態度不明確
example: She felt deep ambivalence about leaving her hometown for a prestigious job overseas.

definition_en: something out of its proper historical time; an outdated element
definition_zh: 時代錯誤；不合時宜的事物
example: A knight using a smartphone in the film was an obvious anachronism that critics found jarring.

definition_en: strong hostility or ill will toward someone
example: The prosecutor's animus toward the defendant was so apparent that the judge issued a warning.

definition_en: the abandonment or renunciation of a religious or political belief
definition_zh: 叛教；放棄信仰；背棄原則
example: His colleagues viewed his sudden support for the opposing party as political apostasy.

definition_en: to pacify someone by conceding to their demands
example: The government attempted to appease protesters by promising immediate policy reviews.

definition_en: very appropriate or pertinent to a particular situation
example: Her comment about honesty was particularly apropos given the recent scandal.

definition_en: understood by only a few; requiring secret or specialized knowledge
definition_zh: 神秘的；晦澀的；只有少數人能理解的
example: The tax code is so arcane that even experienced accountants must consult specialists.

definition_en: intense enthusiasm or passion for a cause or activity
example: The young activist pursued environmental reform with an ardor that inspired her entire community.

definition_en: to claim or seize something without justification
definition_zh: 擅自佔有；霸佔；無理索取
example: The military commander arrogated powers that the constitution reserved solely for civilian authorities.

definition_en: an attack on someone's reputation or integrity; a damaging remark
example: The candidate refused to cast aspersions on his rival, choosing to focus on policy instead.

definition_en: harshness of tone or manner; roughness
example: She responded to the rude journalist with unexpected asperity, silencing the room.

definition_en: to waste away or decline due to lack of use or nourishment
example: Without regular intellectual challenges, critical thinking skills can gradually atrophy.

definition_en: a strong dislike or disinclination toward something
example: His aversion to conflict made him avoid necessary but difficult conversations with his team.

definition_en: lacking moral principles; ignoble or dishonest
definition_zh: 卑鄙的；低劣的；無道德的
example: The whistleblower exposed the base motives behind the executive's charitable donations.

definition_en: to make supremely happy; to declare someone blessed
definition_zh: 使極樂；宣布……為真福者；使神聖化
example: The community seemed to beatify the founder, overlooking all his personal failings.

definition_en: to charm or enchant, sometimes in a deceptive way
example: The con artist beguiled investors with smooth talk and fabricated success stories.

definition_en: to make someone or something seem unimportant or lesser
example: A good mentor never belittles a student's early attempts, no matter how flawed.

definition_en: the act of cutting off or the natural separation of parts
example: The abscission of leaves in autumn is triggered by changes in daylight and temperature.

definition_en: gradual growth or accumulation by addition of layers or matter
example: The accretion of sediment over centuries formed the delta at the river's mouth.

definition_en: to defraud or cheat someone, especially of money
example: The con artist bilked elderly investors out of their retirement savings.

definition_en: flattery or pleasing words used to coax or persuade someone
example: Despite the salesman's blandishments, she refused to purchase the overpriced warranty.

definition_en: to make false and defamatory statements about someone
example: The tabloid routinely calumniated celebrities to drive up circulation.

definition_en: tending to find fault or raise petty objections
example: The captious editor rejected the manuscript over minor stylistic inconsistencies.

definition_en: to make petty or unnecessary objections
example: Instead of addressing the core argument, he caviled about the footnote formatting.

definition_en: stubbornly or willfully disobedient to authority
example: The contumacious defendant repeatedly interrupted the judge and refused to comply with court orders.

definition_en: feeling or expressing remorse for one's wrongdoing
example: The contrite employee apologized sincerely for the error and promised it would not recur.

definition_en: a confusing and difficult problem or question with no clear answer
example: How to balance economic growth with environmental protection remains a persistent conundrum for policymakers.

definition_en: to pamper or treat with excessive indulgence
example: Critics argued that cosseting students by lowering academic standards ultimately harms them.

definition_en: a forceful and bitter verbal attack against someone or something
example: His opening remarks quickly became a diatribe against the opposition's economic policies.

definition_en: spread out over a large area; not concise; wordy
example: The professor's diffuse lecture touched on dozens of topics without fully developing any of them.

definition_en: digressing from subject to subject; not keeping to the main point
definition_zh: 散漫的；東拉西扯的；推論性的
example: Her discursive essay wandered through philosophy, history, and personal memoir without a clear thesis.

definition_en: lack of harmony or agreement; a tension between conflicting elements
example: The cognitive dissonance between his stated values and his actions became impossible to ignore.

definition_en: deceitfulness; the practice of saying one thing while meaning another
definition_zh: 口是心非；兩面三刀；欺詐
example: The diplomat's duplicity was exposed when his private communications contradicted his public statements.

definition_en: a departure from what is normal or expected
example: The scientist dismissed the unusual result as a statistical aberration rather than a meaningful finding.

definition_en: of doubtful authenticity, although widely circulated as true
definition_zh: 真實性可疑的；偽造的；廣傳但未經證實的
example: The story of the general's midnight ride is likely apocryphal, invented decades after his death.

definition_en: clever or cunning devices or expedients, especially as used to trick or deceive
example: The con artist relied on artifice and charm to gain the trust of wealthy investors.

definition_en: having an ability to accurately assess situations and people; shrewd
example: An astute investor, she recognized the company's potential long before it became profitable.

definition_en: relating to a reversion to something ancient or ancestral
definition_zh: 返祖的；隔代遺傳的；回歸原始本能的
example: The sociologist argued that tribalism reflects an atavistic impulse that modern institutions have failed to extinguish.

definition_en: sternness or severity; difficult economic conditions due to reduced spending
definition_zh: 嚴苛；緊縮；樸素（尤指經濟緊縮）
example: Years of government austerity left public hospitals chronically underfunded and overwhelmed.

definition_en: in a state of intellectual or moral ignorance
definition_zh: 愚昧的；無知的；道德上落後的
example: The reformers viewed the rural villages as benighted communities in need of education and enlightenment.

definition_en: to talk in a loud, aggressive, or indignant way with little effect
definition_zh: 虛張聲勢；大聲恐嚇；空洞威脅
example: The manager blustered about consequences, but the employees knew he lacked the authority to follow through.

definition_en: high-sounding but with little meaning; inflated in speech
definition_zh: 誇大其詞的；浮誇的；言過其實的
example: The candidate's bombastic rhetoric impressed supporters but alienated undecided voters looking for substantive policy.

definition_en: a thing that is helpful or beneficial; a blessing
example: The new direct train service was a boon for commuters who had previously spent hours transferring between lines.

definition_en: to lower in rank, prestige, or esteem; to humiliate
example: The dictator sought to abase his political rivals by publicly mocking their achievements.

definition_en: utterly hopeless or degraded; contemptibly low
definition_zh: 極度悲慘的；卑微的；可憐的
example: The refugees lived in abject poverty, lacking even basic necessities.

definition_en: moderate and sparing, especially in eating and drinking
definition_zh: 節制的；有節度的（尤指飲食方面）
example: Her abstemious habits allowed her to maintain excellent health well into old age.

definition_en: to agree or give consent, often under pressure; to assume an office
example: After lengthy negotiations, the board finally acceded to the workers' demands.

definition_en: an upward slope or incline
example: The hikers struggled up the steep acclivity as the trail grew increasingly difficult.

definition_en: refusing to be persuaded or to change one's mind; unyielding
definition_zh: 堅定不移的；固執的；不可動搖的
example: Despite his family's objections, he remained adamant about pursuing a career in the arts.

definition_en: clever or skillful in using the hands or mind
example: The adroit negotiator managed to secure favorable terms for both parties.

definition_en: coming from an external or unexpected source; not inherent
definition_zh: 外來的；偶發的；非本質的
example: The scholar dismissed the new evidence as adventitious and irrelevant to the core argument.

definition_en: to publicly support or recommend a cause or policy
example: The senator has long advocated for comprehensive immigration reform.

definition_en: friendly, good-natured, and easy to talk to
definition_zh: 和藹可親的；平易近人的
example: Her affable manner made her an instant favorite among the new colleagues.

definition_en: the act of increasing one's own power, wealth, or importance
definition_zh: 自我擴張；誇大；提高地位
example: Critics accused the CEO of using company funds purely for personal aggrandizement.

definition_en: to diminish or put to rest fear, suspicion, or worry
definition_zh: 減輕（恐懼、憂慮）；緩和；消除
example: The doctor's calm explanation helped allay the patient's fears about the procedure.

definition_en: diametrically opposite in nature or character
example: Their political views were antipodal, making meaningful dialogue nearly impossible.

definition_en: directly opposed or contrasted; mutually incompatible
example: Greed is antithetical to the spirit of charity that defines this organization.

definition_en: a short, witty, and instructive saying; a maxim
example: Benjamin Franklin's apothegms, such as 'a penny saved is a penny earned,' remain widely quoted today.

definition_en: enthusiastic or passionate; intensely devoted
example: She was an ardent supporter of environmental reform throughout her career.

definition_en: the jargon or slang of a particular group or class
definition_zh: 行話；黑話；特定群體的術語
example: The detective had to learn the criminal argot to understand the intercepted messages.

definition_en: the reappearance of ancestral characteristics after several generations of absence
definition_zh: 返祖現象；隔代遺傳；舊習復萌
example: The sociologist described the resurgence of tribalism as a form of cultural atavism.

definition_en: modesty or shyness resulting from a lack of self-confidence
example: Despite her brilliant research, her diffidence prevented her from presenting at major conferences.

definition_en: to wear away by friction; to irritate or erode
example: Years of wind and sand had abraded the ancient inscriptions until they were barely legible.

definition_en: self-confident composure, especially under pressure
example: She handled the hostile questions from the panel with remarkable aplomb.

definition_en: producing an anticlimax; disappointingly trivial after something grand
example: The film's bathetic ending disappointed critics who had expected a powerful conclusion.

definition_en: swiftness of action or motion
example: The crisis demanded that the committee act with unusual celerity.

definition_en: to give off flashes of light; to sparkle brilliantly
example: The wit in his essays coruscates on every page, dazzling even skeptical readers.

definition_en: to raise objections or show reluctance
example: She demurred when asked to sign the contract without first consulting her lawyer.

definition_en: to make someone feel uneasy or embarrassed; to thwart
example: The unexpected question visibly discomfited the spokesperson during the press conference.

definition_en: mental calmness and composure, especially in difficult situations
example: Despite the sudden loss of funding, the research team accepted the news with equanimity.

definition_en: requiring immediate attention; pressing and urgent
example: The flooding created an exigent situation that demanded an immediate government response.

definition_en: silly and pointless; complacently foolish
definition_zh: 愚蠢的；自以為是的蠢話
example: His fatuous remarks about the economy revealed how little he understood the subject.

definition_en: attempting to avoid notice; secretive and stealthy
definition_zh: 鬼鬼祟祟的；偷偷摸摸的
example: She cast a furtive glance at her competitor's notes during the debate preparation.

definition_en: deserving or causing public disgrace; humiliating
example: The once-celebrated politician suffered an ignominious defeat in his bid for re-election.

definition_en: unchanging over time; unable to be altered
example: Scientists once considered the laws of thermodynamics to be immutable truths of nature.

definition_en: showing casual lack of concern; carefree and nonchalant
definition_zh: 漫不經心的；無憂無慮的
example: Her insouciant attitude toward deadlines frustrated her meticulous colleagues.

definition_en: clear and transparent; easily understood
example: The philosopher's limpid prose made even the most abstruse concepts accessible to general readers.

definition_en: inefficient or clumsy, especially in social situations
example: His maladroit attempt at humor only deepened the tension in the room.

definition_en: to make unclear or confusing; to bewilder deliberately
definition_zh: 使模糊；使困惑；故意混淆
example: The report's dense jargon seemed designed to obfuscate rather than illuminate the findings.

definition_en: full of danger or uncertainty; perilous
definition_zh: 危險的；艱難的；岌岌可危的
example: The startup found itself in a parlous financial state after its main investor withdrew.

definition_en: in a state of quietness or inactivity; dormant
definition_zh: 靜止的；休眠的；不活躍的
example: The volcano had been quiescent for two centuries before it suddenly erupted.

definition_en: to refuse to accept or be associated with; to deny the truth of
definition_zh: 否認；拒絕接受；與……劃清界限
example: The scientist publicly repudiated the conclusions drawn from her misquoted research.

definition_en: having a gloomy, sullen, or morose temperament
definition_zh: 憂鬱的；陰沉的；悶悶不樂的
example: His saturnine demeanor made it difficult for colleagues to approach him with light-hearted conversation.

definition_en: a beginner or novice in a particular field
example: Even a tyro in linguistics could detect the grammatical errors riddling the poorly edited manuscript.

definition_en: attractive or appealing in a fresh, innocent way
definition_zh: 迷人的；可愛的；討人喜歡的
example: Her winsome smile and genuine curiosity endeared her to everyone she interviewed.

definition_en: playful, teasing conversation or banter
definition_zh: 輕鬆的戲謔對話；玩笑話
example: The two colleagues exchanged witty badinage before the meeting began.

definition_en: inexperienced and immature
example: The callow intern struggled to handle the pressure of the fast-paced newsroom.

definition_en: to unfairly criticize or belittle someone or something
example: The politician tried to denigrate his opponent's record rather than address the issues.

definition_en: insolent boldness or shameless audacity
example: She had the effrontery to demand a refund after using the product for six months.

definition_en: highly productive or fertile, especially intellectually
example: The fecund author published three acclaimed novels in a single decade.

definition_en: to skip or leap about playfully
example: Lambs gamboled in the meadow as spring arrived.

definition_en: leadership or dominant influence of one group over others
example: The nation sought to maintain its cultural hegemony over the region.

definition_en: to dispute the truth or validity of; to call into question
definition_zh: 質疑；駁斥；抨擊（某人的動機或誠信）
example: The defense attorney sought to impugn the credibility of the witness.

definition_en: naive, simplistic, or lacking in substance
definition_zh: 幼稚的；貧乏的；缺乏深度的
example: The critic dismissed the essay as jejune, offering nothing beyond obvious observations.

definition_en: one's range of knowledge or understanding
example: Quantum mechanics lies beyond the ken of most non-specialists.

definition_en: occupying a transitional or intermediate position or state
definition_zh: 過渡性的；處於臨界狀態的
example: The weeks between graduation and starting a new job felt like a liminal period of uncertainty.

definition_en: expressing or conveying a threat; menacing
example: The general's minatory tone left no doubt that he intended to use force if necessary.

definition_en: of no value or importance; trifling or ineffective
definition_zh: 無價值的；微不足道的；無效的
example: The amendment was nugatory, doing nothing to address the core problem.

definition_en: to become rigid, inflexible, or resistant to change
definition_zh: 僵化；固化；使思想或制度變得頑固不化
example: Without reform, the institution risks ossifying into irrelevance.

definition_en: expressing contempt or disapproval; having a belittling connotation
definition_zh: 貶義的；輕蔑的；帶有負面含義的
example: The term was originally neutral but has since acquired a pejorative meaning.

definition_en: habitually complaining; whining or fretful
definition_zh: 愛抱怨的；牢騷滿腹的；易發怒的
example: The querulous patient complained about everything from the food to the temperature of the room.

definition_en: promoting health or well-being; healthful
definition_zh: 有益健康的；衛生的；宜人的
example: The doctor recommended a move to a more salubrious climate to aid her recovery.

definition_en: swollen or pompous; excessively ornate in style
definition_zh: 浮誇的；腫脹的；文風過度華麗的
example: The turgid prose of the nineteenth-century treatise made it nearly unreadable.

definition_en: a feeling of offense or resentment
definition_zh: 不快；憤慨；因受冒犯而生的怨恨
example: She took umbrage at the suggestion that her research was derivative.

definition_en: remaining as a trace of something that has largely disappeared
definition_zh: 殘留的；退化的；已消退但仍有痕跡的
example: The vestigial appendix no longer serves a digestive function in humans.

definition_en: deliberate and unprovoked; showing no restraint or consideration
definition_zh: 恣意的；無端的；放縱的；肆意妄為的
example: The wanton destruction of historical artifacts outraged the international community.

definition_en: an intense dislike or fear of people from other countries or cultures
definition_zh: 排外心理；對外國人或外來文化的恐懼與憎惡
example: Rising xenophobia threatened to undermine the country's tradition of welcoming immigrants.

definition_en: fanatical and uncompromising pursuit of ideals
definition_zh: 狂熱主義；盲目偏執的信仰追求
example: The movement's early idealism gradually curdled into dangerous zealotry.

definition_en: something that transforms or refines, as if by distillation
definition_zh: 蒸餾器；（比喻）提煉或淨化的媒介
example: The editorial process served as an alembic, refining raw ideas into polished prose.

definition_en: showing fearlessness and determination
example: The dauntless explorer pressed on despite the treacherous mountain conditions.

definition_en: a state of disuse or obsolescence
example: Many of the factory's machines had fallen into desuetude after the switch to automation.

definition_en: not candid or sincere, typically by pretending ignorance
definition_zh: 不坦誠的；虛偽的；假裝無知的
example: It was disingenuous of the executive to claim he had no knowledge of the scandal.

definition_en: open to more than one interpretation; ambiguous or uncertain
definition_zh: 模稜兩可的；含糊不清的
example: The scientist's equivocal findings left the research community unsure how to proceed.

definition_en: inactive; not in use; (of land) left unseeded to restore fertility
definition_zh: 休耕的；閒置的；未開發的
example: After the bestseller, she let her creative energies lie fallow for two years.

definition_en: intensely enthusiastic or passionate
example: His fervid advocacy for prison reform moved many legislators to act.

definition_en: stubbornly contrary and disobedient
example: The froward student refused every attempt by teachers to guide his behavior.

definition_en: a person who enjoys eating and often eats too much
definition_zh: 貪食者；美食愛好者（帶有過度之意）
example: A self-described gourmand, he documented every extravagant meal on his blog.

definition_en: not conforming to accepted or orthodox standards or beliefs
definition_zh: 異端的；非正統的；非傳統的
example: Her heterodox economic theories were ridiculed before eventually being vindicated.

definition_en: unable to be affected by; not allowing something to pass through
definition_zh: 不受影響的；無法穿透的
example: He seemed impervious to criticism, continuing his work with calm confidence.

definition_en: unable to be resisted or avoided; inescapable
definition_zh: 不可避免的；無法逃脫的
example: The decline of the empire seemed ineluctable given its internal corruption.

definition_en: to praise highly, especially in a public context
example: Critics lauded the debut novel as the most important work of the decade.

definition_en: apparently attractive but having no real value; showy
definition_zh: 華而不實的；表面吸引人但毫無價值的
example: The meretricious packaging concealed a product of remarkably poor quality.

definition_en: having an extremely offensive smell; harmful or noxious
definition_zh: 惡臭的；有害的；令人厭惡的
example: The noisome fumes from the factory drove residents from their homes.

definition_en: to make an offense or illness less severe without removing the cause
definition_zh: 緩解；減輕（病症或罪責）；掩飾
example: The medication could only palliate the symptoms rather than cure the disease.

definition_en: bitterness or ill feeling harbored over a long time
example: Years of rancor between the two families made any reconciliation seem impossible.

definition_en: showing dedication and diligent care
definition_zh: 孜孜不倦的；勤勉的；認真細心的
example: The sedulous researcher spent years verifying each claim in the manuscript.

definition_en: wickedness or depravity of character or behavior
example: The official was disbarred for moral turpitude after the fraud was uncovered.
